mergeRationale: The early-return trusts a client-settable header (x-middleware-subrequest), so any unauthenticated request can set it and skip the auth middleware guarding protected routes.

requiredFindings.summary: The patch trusts x-middleware-subrequest read straight from the incoming request.
requiredFindings.expectedReasoning: x-middleware-subrequest is an ordinary request header; an unauthenticated external request can set it and hit the early-return, skipping the middleware that protects /admin, /billing and /settings.
requiredFindings.acceptableFix: Do not trust this header inside app middleware; upgrade Next.js to a fixed version and strip/reject internal headers at the edge or reverse proxy, plus re-check authorization in route handlers.

requiredFindings.summary: The new test only proves the early-return branch exists; it never asserts a spoofed request is blocked.
requiredFindings.expectedReasoning: The test sets the header and expects a 200. It does not assert that an unauthenticated request to /admin carrying a forged x-middleware-subrequest is still redirected/blocked, so the dangerous path stays green.
requiredFindings.acceptableFix: Add a negative test: unauthenticated request to a protected route with a spoofed x-middleware-subrequest header must still be redirected/blocked.

optionalFindings.summary: Route handlers should not rely solely on middleware for high-value authorization checks.

disallowedConclusions: The patch is safe because the header is used by the framework.
Only checking the exact header value is enough.
